ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai hot and sour fried rice; closest parent 'fried rice food product' (FOODON_00002694) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Fried rice) is the closest parent category; no specific Thai hot and sour fried rice code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Thai hot and sour fried rice or fried rice as a prepared dish at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of hot and sour fried rice is approximately 300 g, equivalent to one restaurant plate as typically served in Singapore Thai restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, Thai-style — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table composite estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai hot and sour fried rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Rice, fried, Chinese restaurant' (FDC ID 169957) and FAO/INFOODS SEA fried rice composite, adjusted for the hot-and-sour flavour profile (additional lime juice, chilli, fish sauce) which marginally increases sodium and reduces fat compared to standard Chinese fried rice. Protein reflects optional meat inclusion at moderate proportion. All values represent a plausible mid-range estimate for a restaurant-prepared Thai hot and sour fried rice with meat.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — Fried rice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai hot and sour fried rice. GI of 64 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice-based fried rice (GI approximately 64, medium category), consistent with Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The hot-and-sour flavour additions (lime juice, chilli, fish sauce) do not materially alter the glycemic response of the rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 79.5) / 100 ≈ 51.0, based on 300 g serving with 26.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, soy sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (approximately 1440 mg per standard 300 g serving), which represents over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced fish sauce and no added salt when ordering is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from white jasmine rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish as the white jasmine rice base produces a medium glycemic index of approximately 64 and a high glycemic load of approximately 51 per standard serving, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion reduction to 150 g and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables or protein is recommended to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium from stir-frying with oil and fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation, as the combination of approximately 1.0 g saturated fat per 100 g and high sodium (~480 mg per 100 g) from fish sauce and seasoning may contribute to adverse cardiovascular outcomes if consumed frequently; choosing lean meat options and requesting reduced oil and sauce preparation is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot and Sour Fried Rice (Thai: ข้าวผัดเปรี้ยวเผ็ด, romanized: Khao Phat Priao Phet) is a Thai stir-fried rice dish characterised by a bold combination of sour and spicy flavours. It is prepared by wok-frying cooked jasmine rice with egg, garlic, chilli, lime juice, fish sauce, and spring onion in vegetable oil over high heat. Meat options such as chicken or pork are commonly added. The dish delivers a fragrant, slightly smoky aroma from wok hei, with a tangy sourness from lime juice and a sharp heat from fresh chilli balanced by the umami depth of fish sauce. The texture is light and slightly chewy from the rice grains, with tender egg and meat pieces throughout. In Singapore, this dish is served in Thai restaurants and casual dining establishments, often as a main course. Nutritionally, a standard 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 504 kcal, 17.4 g protein, 13.5 g fat, and 79.5 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is high at approximately 1440 mg per serving due to fish sauce and seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The white jasmine rice base produces a medium glycemic index (~64) and a high glycemic load (~51 per serving), which is clinically significant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free but contains egg and fish allergens. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans in its standard form.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai hot and sour fried rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried rice data and FAO/INFOODS SEA composite values, adjusted for the Thai hot-and-sour flavour profile. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using an analogous white rice-based fried rice entry. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. The restaurant name 'Pashuu' in the input food name appears to be a brand or restaurant-specific descriptor (The Upper House - Funan, Singapore); the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Hot and Sour Fried Rice' with 'Pashuu' retained as an alias. 2. Meat options are available per the input description; nutrient values reflect a moderate meat inclusion (chicken or pork) as the mid-range scenario. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy fried rice source entry. 4. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy fried rice source entry. 5. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy fried rice source entry. 6. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is a confirmed near-zero value consistent with vegetable oil-based stir-frying without hydrogenated fats, confirmed per USDA SR Legacy data for comparable fried rice preparations. 7. The halal status is marked false as fish sauce of uncertain halal certification is a standard ingredient; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 8. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice-based fried rice; no direct measurement for this specific dish exists in the approved GI literature.
